coredata.dc:title: A label-free fluorescent sensor based on yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of congo red and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lizhen
Mi, Zhi
Wang, Junling
Liu, Zhixiong
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an innovative label-free fluorescent sensor based on yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots (YG-CQDs) was first constructed to determine congo red with high speed and sensitivity. The YG-CQDs were simply and quickly prepared using p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors via a hydrothermal method. The prepared YG-CQDs exhibits favorable water solubility and excellent photoluminescence. The YG-CQDs showed an obvious fluorescence quenching behavior as the congo red content increase duo to an inner filter effect (IFE). YG-CQDs served as an effective fluorescent sensor in detecting congo red with high selectivity and sensitivity in a fast and direct manner under optimal conditions. Two good linear relationships were obtained for congo red detection over the concentration ranges of 0.5–50 and 50–170 μg mL−1, corresponding to two lower detection limits of 0.04 and 0.03 μg mL−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the constructed fluorescent sensor was successfully adopted to determine congo red in tap and lake water samples where the accuracy was high. In addition, due to low toxicity and superior biocompatibility, YG-CQDs was also found to be an effective agent for cellular imaging with satisfactory results. The results reveal the good potential of the prepared YG-CQDs for congo red sensing and cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots
Label-free fluorescent sensor
Congo red
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the YG-CQDs to congo red
Analysis of congo red in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province
Doctoral Science Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciated the support of Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434) and the Doctoral Science Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University (2016-B-13 and 2014-B-13).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of YG-CQDs. Inset: HR-TEM image. (B) Size distribution of YG-CQDs.
XPS spectra of YG-CQDs. (A) Full survey spectra, (B) high-resolution C1s XPS spectra, (C) high-resolution Ο1s XPS spectra and (D) high-resolution Ν1s XPS spectra.

(A) UV absorption spectra of the YG-CQDs solution. Inset: photograph of the YG-CQDs solution under daylight lamp (right) and UV–visible light lamp (left). (B) Excitation (blue line) and emission (green line) spectra of the YG-CQDs solution. (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of YG-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (330–440 nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of YG-CQDs at different congo red concentrations (0–170 µg/mL). (B) and (C) are the linear relationships between F0/F and the different concentrations of congo red (0.5–50 µg/mL and 50–170 µg/mL). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Fluorescence response of YG-CQDs in the presence of congo red (10 µg/mL), metal ions (1 mg/mL), anions (1 mg/mL) and other dyes (1 mg/mL). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
Cytotoxicity test of YG-CQDs on Hela cells viability. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Laser scanning confocal microscope images of Hela cells treated with only culture medium (control) and 0.3 mg/mL YG-CQDs (sample) for 4 h. (A) and (E) are bright filed images; (B) and (F) are cell images at excitation wavelength of 405 nm; (C) and (G) are cell images at excitation wavelength of 559 nm; (D) and (H) are the merged images of (B) and (C) and (F) and (G), respectively.
Analytical results of congo red in real sample (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A label-free fluorescent sensor based on yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of congo red and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an innovative label-free fluorescent sensor based on yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots (YG-CQDs) was first constructed to determine congo red with high speed and sensitivity. The YG-CQDs were simply and quickly prepared using p-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors via a hydrothermal method. The prepared YG-CQDs exhibits favorable water solubility and excellent photoluminescence. The YG-CQDs showed an obvious fluorescence quenching behavior as the congo red content increase duo to an inner filter effect (IFE). YG-CQDs served as an effective fluorescent sensor in detecting congo red with high selectivity and sensitivity in a fast and direct manner under optimal conditions. Two good linear relationships were obtained for congo red detection over the concentration ranges of 0.5–50 and 50–170 μg mL−1, corresponding to two lower detection limits of 0.04 and 0.03 μg mL−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the constructed fluorescent sensor was successfully adopted to determine congo red in tap and lake water samples where the accuracy was high. In addition, due to low toxicity and superior biocompatibility, YG-CQDs was also found to be an effective agent for cellular imaging with satisfactory results. The results reveal the good potential of the prepared YG-CQDs for congo red sensing and cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Congo red refers to an anionic diazo dye, which is formed by coupling tetrazotised benzidine and two naphthionic acid molecules (Fig. S1). Congo red is often used in cosmetics, printing, paper, food, leather and textile industries [1,2]. Congo red dyeing process requires a lot of water consumption. Wastewater containing congo red has the characteristics of difficult biodegradation, serious pollution, and high in organic and color content, because of the complicated aromatic structure of congo red [3–5]. In addition, congo red was found to be highly toxic, and its metabolite, benzidine, is a well-known carcinogen that can cause cancer and mutagenesis in living organisms [6]. Due to the above properties, the environment and human health are subject to various hazards and major risks. Currently, some analytical methods have been put forward to determine congo red, such as high-performance liquid chromatography with ultra violet detector (HPLC-UV) [7,8], UV–vis spectrophotometry [9,10] and electrochemical methods [11]. Yet, the majority is not only time-consuming, complicated to operate and poor in selectivity and sensitivity, but also require professional operations and expensive instruments. Therefore, a simple, cost-effective, and rapid method with high sensitivity and accuracy is urgently needed to determine congo red.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials, the size of which is less than 10 nm. They are mainly composed of amorphous carbon and nanocrystalline region of sp2 hybrid graphite structure, and other elements (such as oxygen, hydrogen and nitrogen) may exist at the edges [12–14]. In 2004, Xu et al. first reported CQDs [15]. Since then, it has attracted extensive research interest due to its good water solubility, high chemical inertness, excellent optical properties, low cost and easy functionalization [16]. Especially the good biocompatibility and low toxicity of CQDs make it a good substitute for traditional heavy metal-based quantum dots and organic dyes. So far, CQDs have been applied to a wide range of fields including sensors [17–19], security [20], bioimaging [21,22], photocatalysis [23], drug delivery [24], fluorescent inks [25,26] and optoelectronic devices [27,28]. CQDs can be obtained by many synthetic methods including hydrothermal [29,30], pyrolysis [31], ultrasonic [32], microwave treatment [33], electrochemical oxidation [34] and chemical ablation methods [35]. However, many of these methods have some disadvantages, such as low product yield, limited spectral efficiency, the use of toxic chemicals or high temperature in the experiment [36]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal treatment method has the advantages of simple preparation, easy control of the reaction, low cost and green [37]. Although significant progress has been made in the development of CQDs using various synthesis methods, CQDs synthesized by these methods often emit short-wavelength blue light [38,39], which not only largely affects CQDs' sensitivity to applications because of the lack of a large stokes shift or being susceptible to interference from solvent scattering spectra [40], but also greatly limit their application in biology due to their poor penetration ability as well as serious light damage to biological tissues [41,42]. In addition, some CQDs that emit blue or green light are accompanied by excitation-dependent emission [43–45], but the excitation-dependent emission of these blue or green CQDs is usually attributed to the different photon absorption mechanism of CQDs and they often delivered very low fluorescence quantum yield (ΦS ) for long-wavelength emission light [42,46,47]. Therefore, the development of CQDs with single long-wavelength emission is critical to expand CQDs application.

Herein, a yellow-green emissive carbon quantum dots (YG-CQDs) was fabricated by a simple one-step hydrothermal method, in which the raw materials were p-phenylenediamine and citric acid. The prepared YG-CQDs emit strong yellow-green fluorescence and have the advantages of excellent water solubility, good photostability, superior biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence of YG-CQDs was found being dramatically quenched by congo red through the inner filter effect (IFE). On this basis, a novel fluorescent sensor was constructed, which can be directly used for congo red detection. The proposed sensor was used to determine congo red in tap and lake water samples, showing excellent selectivity and high sensitivity to congo red. To the best of our knowledge, this report first presents the use of YG-CQDs as a fluorescent sensor to detection congo red in a rapid, cheap, simple and sensitive way. In addition, the YG-CQDs has been used for cell imaging of human cervical carcinoma Hela cells because of its good biocompatibility and low toxicity. It was found that YG-CQDs successfully entered the cell membrane and had satisfactory imaging results. The above findings reveal the great potential of the prepared YG-CQDs in sensing and bioimaging.

Quinine sulfate was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and p-phenylenediamine were provided by Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). KBr, HCl, Na2HPO4, NaOH, NaH2PO4, KCl, BaCl2, MgSO4, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, MnCl2, AlCl3, CuSO4, NaNO3 and Na2SO4 were provided by Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China). Paraformaldehyde, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were bought from Xi'an Zhongtuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Xi'an, China). Malachite green, congo red, safranine T and rhodamine B were bought from Chengdu Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). All reagents are analytically pure and can be used directly without treatment. Ultrapure water with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm was applied to the whole experiment. We prepared 5 mg/mL congo red stock solution in ultrapure water and stored it in dark. The acidity of the solution was controlled by 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS).

A Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to obtain the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of YG-CQDs, which ranged 400–3750 cm−1. To prepare KBr tablet, several milligrams of YG-CQDs samples were mixed with approximately 100 mg of KBr. An agate mortar was used to grind them to approximately 2 µm. Then, a pressure of 29.8 MPa was applied. A Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA) was used to collect transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of YG-CQDs at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV–vis measurements of YG-CQDs were done by a Lambda 25 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, UAS) in the range of 200–700 nm. Under the support of a QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, USA), the fluorescence measurements of YG-CQDs were completed. We conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of YG-CQDs and cellular imaging via an Axis Ultra XPS spectrometer (Krato, UK) and a TCS SP8 laser scanning confocal microscopy (Leica, Germany), respectively. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured using a NanoZS instrument (Malvern, UK).

The one-step hydrothermal method used to prepare YG-CQDs is illustrated as follows: we first dissolved 0.5 g of p-phenylenediamine and 0.5 g of citric acid in 20 mL ultrapure water, followed by the 10 min treatment of ultrasound. After being transferred to a 25 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, the mixture was heated at 200 ℃ for 8 h and then cooled to room temperature. Afterward, the solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for 48 h and filtered with a filter membrane (0.45 μm) for removing large particles. Then, we dialyzed the filtrate against ultrapure water for 3 days with a dialysis bag (500 Da) to purify YG-CQDs solution. After freeze-drying, YG-CQDs powder was obtained for further use.

The congo red detection was performed: 0.3 mL of YG-CQDs solution (10 mg mL−1) was mixed with various amounts of congo red solution and diluted to 3 mL with 0.01 mM PBS (pH = 5.0). The mixed solution was mixed thoroughly and equilibrated at room temperature for 5 min. Afterward, when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, we recorded the fluorescence spectra of the mixture in the wavelength range of 380–700 nm. Emission and excitation slits were both set to 10 nm. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) was calculated as a response signal of congo red to YG-CQDs, where F0/F, F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of YG-CQDs without and with congo red, respectively. Possible interferents such as representative metal ions (Cd2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+), common anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 –) and dyes (malachite green, rhodamine B and safranine T) were selected to test the selectivity of YG-CQDs to congo red under the same conditions.

The cytotoxicity of YG-CQDs was assessed by MTT assay on human cervical carcinoma Hela cells. Briefly, a 96 well plate was used to seed HeLa cells, which were then incubated in 100 μL medium (DMEM containing 10% FBS) per well at 37 ℃ with 5% CO2 overnight. Afterward, we discarded the old medium and incubated the HeLa cells in a fresh medium under various concentrations of YG-CQDs (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8 and 1 mg mL−1) for 12 h and 24 h, respectively. The control group was incubated with medium without YG-CQDs. After being added into each well, 50 μL MTT solution was incubated for next 4 h, and 150 μL DMSO was added into each well after the removal of the medium with MTT. The plate was shaken for about 10 min. Optical density (OD) was measured using the Spark Multimode Microplate Reader (Tecan, Switzerland) at 490 nm. Cell viability was calculated as:
Cell viability (%) = (ODT/ODC)
where ODT and ODC were obtained presence and in absence of YG-CQDs, respectively.

A confocal dish was used to culture HeLa cells at a concentration of 8 × 104 mL−1 with 5% CO2 at 37 ℃ until the cells adhered to the wall, followed by 4 h of incubation with YG-CQDs (0.3 mg/mL). The control group was incubated without YG-CQDs. Then PBS was used to wash the cells three times for removing excessive YG-CQDs. The cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. Finally, at an excitation wavelength of 405 and 559 nm, cell images were recorded by a laser confocal scanning microscope.

TEM test was conducted for characterizing the particle size and morphology of the prepared YG-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , YG-CQDs is uniformly dispersed and has nearly spherical shape. The size distribution histogram was drawn by randomly counting about 100 YG-CQDs particles on the TEM image. The size distribution range and the average diameter of YG-CQDs is 1.0–4.5 nm and 2.3 nm, respectively. The high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image exhibits a lattice fringe spacing of approximately 0.21 nm (the inset of Fig. 1A), which is attributed to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene structure (100 facet) [48]. In addition, DLS study has been performed to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of the prepared YG-CQDs. As displayed by Fig. S2, the distribution range of hydrodynamic diameter is 1.6–7.6 nm and the average hydrodynamic diameter is 4.1 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter is larger than the dry-state diameter estimated by TEM, which may be due to the different states of the YG-CQDs under the two measurement conditions. In the TEM test, the water solvent is removed, while for DLS, the sample is dissolved in the water solvent and water molecules are attracted to the surface of the YG-CQDs, resulting a larger diameter than that of TEM [49,50].

XPS and FT-IR were used to determine YG-CQDs' structure, surface functional groups and elemental composition. FT-IR spectrum of YG-CQDs is depicted in Fig. S3, where the characteristic stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H are embodied in the absorption peaks at 3430 and 3221 cm−1, respectively. The existence of these functional groups makes the YG-CQDs have excellent water solubility. The absorption band at 2930 cm−1 comes from the C–H stretching vibration, while the band centered at 1699 cm−1 can be explained by the C = N/C = O stretching vibration. The band at 1625 cm−1 originates from C = C stretching vibration, indicating the presence of polycyclic aromatic structure. The peaks appearing at 1665, 1517 and 1396 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of Amide Ⅰ,Ⅱ and III, respectively [51–53]. The stretching vibration peaks of C-O appear at 1129 cm−1, while that of C-N is at 830 cm−1. In addition, the XPS spectra of YG-CQDs is displayed in Fig. 2 . There are three distinct peaks of N1s (399.5 eV), O1s (531.6 eV) and C1s (284.8 eV) in the full survey spectra of YG-CQDs (Fig. 2A), which indicates that the synthesized YG-CQDs are mainly composed of C, O and N. To be more specific, C1s XPS spectra with high resolution (Fig. 2B) shows four characteristic peaks of C–C/C = C, C-O/C-N, C = N and C = O at 284.6, 285.5, 287.9 and 289.0 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2C) exhibits three peaks of C = O, C-O and O–H at 531.5, 532.5 and 533.75 eV, respectively. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2D) displays three peaks of pyrrolic N, amino N and pyridinic N at 401.6, 399.9 and 399.4 eV, respectively [54]. The highest content is pyridinic N, followed by amino N and pyrrolic N. The presence of different N-containing functional groups on the surface of YG-CQDs can lead to the introduction of surface states, accompanied by the enhancement of YG-CQDs fluorescence [55]. In addition, the presence of amino groups on the surface of YG-CQDs ensures that the YG-CQDs have good water solubility. The combined results of FT-IR and XPS indicate that YG-CQDs' surface presents abundant amino and oxygen-containing groups, as well as some N-heterocycles such as pyridine ring and pyrrole ring.

UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to explore the optical properties of YG-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . Under a UV lamp (365 nm), the YG-CQDs aqueous solution emits a yellow-green fluorescence, while it appears a brown yellow color under sunlight (Inset of Fig. 3A). Fig. 3A depicts the UV spectra of YG-CQDs. Two distinct absorption peaks at 220–350 and 400–600 nm are observed from the UV spectra. The former originated from π → π* electronic transition of C = C and n → π* electronic transition of C = N/C = O. [56]. The latter corresponds to electronic transition of N-heterocycles on the surface of the YG-CQDs [57–59], which may lead to long wavelength fluorescence emission [59]. The excitation and emission spectra of YG-CQDs were displayed in Fig. 3B. The wavelength position of the maximum emission peak is 500 nm, while that of the maximum excitation wavelength is 360 nm. In addition, at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the ΦS of YG-CQDs is 11.7% as compared to quinine sulfate (Fig. S4). Fig. 3C presents YG-CQDs' fluorescence emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 330 to 440 nm, the emission peak is red-shifted from 489 to 527 nm, with a decreasing intensity. In fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, this excitation-dependent phenomenon is common and may be originated from different sizes of particles in the sample and the surface states formed by various functional groups on each particle [60].

To test the stability of YG-CQDs, the influence of irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of YG-CQDs was investigated and shown in Fig. S5. The fluorescence intensity of YG-CQDs had no remarkable change even after 150 min UV light irradiation. It indicates that YG-CQDs has good stability against photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the YG-CQDs to congo red
ce:section.ce:para: The working conditions not only affect the stability of the constructed sensor, but also affect the sensitivity of the sensor for congo red detection. Therefore, how pH, the amount of YG-CQDs solution and the reaction time affected the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) were explored. The influence of pH on F0/F was firstly investigated and shown by Fig. S6A. When pH value increases from 2 to 10, F0/F fluctuates and reaches maximum at pH 5. The reason may be explained as follows: congo red is a typical anionic dye. Its molecular structure contains two negatively charged functional groups SO3 −. The pKa value of congo red is 4.1. Below pH 5, congo red is mainly in molecular form. At and above pH value of 5, congo red produces polar groups (R-SO3 −). Since the surface of the synthesized YG-CQDs contains a large number of carboxyl and amino functional groups, the YG-CQDs become positively charged under acidic conditions and negatively charged under alkaline conditions. At pH 5, the anionic form of congo red and the protonated surface moieties of YG-CQDs promoted the interaction between them. The further increase in pH supports the presence of anionic congo red specie. However, under high pH conditions, the functional moieties on the YG-CQDs are deprotonated to obtain a negative charge. The interaction between the negatively charged YG-CQDs and the congo red anion becomes weaker. Thus, the subsequent experiment was conducted at pH 5. Then, the influence of YG-CQDs dosage on F0/F was studied, with the results in Fig. S6B. As the dosage of YG-CQDs increases from 0.1 to 0.5 mL, F0/F first gradually increases and then decreases. Since the fluorescence intensity of YG-CQDs (F0) is dosage-dependent (Fig. S7). The relatively low dosage of YG-CQDs lead to too weak fluorescence intensity and higher dosage of YG-CQDs decrease the fluorescence intensity due to self-quenching. The maximum of F0/F is obtained when the dosage of YG-CQDs is 0.3 mL. As such, the following work was carried out with the dosage of YG-CQDs at 0.3 mL. Finally, the influence of reaction time on F0/F was examined and illustrated by Fig. S6C. The F0/F shows an upward trend as the reaction time extends from 0 to 5 min and reaches stable in a longer time range (≥5 min). Consequently, the sensing work selected 5 min as the optimal reaction time. To conclude, pH 5, 0.3 mL YG-CQDs solution (10 mg mL−1) and reaction time of 5 min were used as the optimal working conditions for congo red detection.

Under the above optimal working conditions, YG-CQDs' fluorescence response to congo red was studied and depicted by Fig. 4 A. As the content of congo red in the YG-CQDs solution increases, the fluorescence intensity of the YG-CQDs solution gradually weakens, revealing the efficient role of congo red in quenching YG-CQDs fluorescence. The F0/F was evaluated based on the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation: F0/F = KSV[C] + 1, where [C] represents the congo red concentration and KSV is the SV quenching constant. According to Fig. 4B-C, the SV plots describing F0/F against the congo red concentration show that the F0/F has two linear responses to the concentration of congo red in the ranges of 0.5–50 and 50–170 μg mL−1, respectively. The corresponding KSV are calculated to be 0.0289 and 0.04 mL μg−1, respectively. The limits of detection (LODs) for congo red are 0.04 and 0.03 μg/mL, respectively, as defined by the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3.

We compared constructed sensor with the previously reported methods for congo red detection in terms of analytical performance [8,9,11]. As shown in Table S1, the proposed fluorescence method based on YG-CQDs achieves much lower LOD than other reported methods, which indicates that our developed fluorescence method has high sensitivity in this work. In addition, it is worth mentioning that our proposed YG-CQDs-based fluorescence method is simple, cheap, fast and environmentally friendly, more suitable for the routine analysis of congo red in actual samples.

The quenching of YG-CQDs fluorescence was achieved when congo red existed. In order to further clarify the mechanism, we recorded the UV–vis absorption spectrum of congo red and the fluorescence spectrum of YG-CQDs. As depicted by Fig. S8A, congo red shows two broad absorption peaks between 280 and 620 nm, and the maximum absorption wavelengths are 340 and 500 nm, respectively. YG-CQDs exhibits excitation and emission spectrum in the wavelength range of 310–470 and 380–670 nm, respectively. The excitation and emission wavelengths reach the maximum value of 360 and 500 nm, respectively. It can be seen that absorption spectra of congo red significantly overlap YG-CQDs' excitation and emission spectra, indicating inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) are the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism [61].

In general, FRET shortens the donor’s fluorescent lifetime, whereas IFE does not. Caused by vibrational collisions, non-radiative energy transfer in FRET is therefore limited to 10 nm, while radiative energy transfer in IFE is subject to no distance limits [62]. The above two quenching mechanisms were further distinguished by time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. S8B shows the fluorescence lifetime of YG-CQDs in the absence and presence of congo red at 8.92 and 8.91 ns, respectively. YG-CQDs' fluorescence lifetime was almost unchanged with and without of congo red, further indicating IFE as the main sensing mechanism [63].

To study the selectivity, the fluorescence response of YG-CQDs solution to various possible interference substances, including common metal ions (K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+ and Cu2+), anions (Cl-, SO4 2- and NO3 –) and other dyes (malachite, green rhodamine B and safranine T), were studied. As displayed by Fig. 5 , only congo red caused significant quenching of YG-CQDs' fluorescence intensity, while other possible interfering substances caused little or no change in it. It indicates that YG-CQDs have good selectivity for congo red compared with other interference substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of congo red in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed YG-CQDs-based fluorescence method for actual samples analysis was studied by measuring the congo red content in tap and lake water samples. A 0.22 μm membrane filter was used to filter tap and lake water, and the filtrates were collected in the sample bottle and stored at 4 ℃ for analysis. A known amount of congo red was added to water samples to perform the recovery test. Table 1 presents the recoveries of congo red in the above water samples at 97–103.1%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) at no more than 2.6% (n = 5), indicating the proposed fluorescence sensor based on YG-CQDs is highly accurate and can be applied for congo red detection in actual samples.

The study of biocompatibility of synthesized YG-CQDs is important for its further intracellular imaging applications. Therefore, MTT assay was applied to investigating the cytotoxicity of YG-CQDs [64]. The viability of the Hela cells cultured with YG-CQDs was analyzed, with untreated Hela cells as control. As depicted by Fig. 6 , HeLa cells were incubated with YG-CQDs at various concentrations (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8 and 1 mg mL−1) for 12 h and 24 h, respectively. Even after incubation with ultra-high concentration (1 mg mL−1) of YG-CQDs for 24 h, the cell viability remained more than 90%, indicating that the synthesized YG-CQDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

YG-CQDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be applied to bioimaging. Hela cells are used as model cells for intracellular imaging. Laser scanning confocal microscope was used to image the cells. 4 h incubation of Hela cells with YG-CQDs (0.3 mg mL−1) was performed, and laser scanning confocal microscope was used to record cellular imaging results at an excitation wavelength of 405 and 559 nm, respectively. Hela cells incubated without YG-CQDs were used as control. Fig. 7 displays the laser scanning confocal microscope images of Hela cells that were incubated without and with YG-CQDs. YG-CQDs presented no significant morphological damage, which further confirmed that YG-CQDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. YG-CQDs are internalized and distributed in cell membrane, cytoplasm and nucleus. As shown by Fig. 7B and C, the Hela cells internalized YG-CQDs emitted obvious yellow and red fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 405 and 559 nm, respectively. It displays the strengths of the YG-CQDs compared with other marker reagents, providing more choices for the observation wavelength. The merged images of Fig. 7F and G (Fig. 7H) show that the YG-CQDs have excellent ability to penetrate cell membrane. These observations further confirm that the synthesized YG-CQDs are multicolor photoluminescence, small toxicity and high biocompatibility, thereby enabling them with a promising application prospect in cell imaging.

In this work, YG-CQDs with favorable water solubility and excellent photoluminescence are successfully prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine. A novel YG-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor was first developed to quickly and simply determine congo red. With the increase of congo red content, the obvious fluorescence quenching of YG-CQDs originates from the IFE mechanism between congo red and YG-CQDs. The constructed fluorescent sensor has the advantages of fast response, simplicity, wide linear range, low cost, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. Furthermore, the proposed sensor was used to determine congo red in lake and tap water samples with satisfactory results. More importantly, long-wavelength emission, low toxicity and superior biocompatibility render the YG-CQDs an ideal agent for cellular imaging. It was successfully used for Hela cell imaging. The prepared YG-CQDs not only provides an innovative and convenient tool to determine congo red, but also highlights its potential application in biological systems.

Lizhen Liu: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Software, Writing - review & editing. Zhi Mi: Investigation, Software, Project administration. Junling Wang: Investigation, Software. Zhixiong Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Feng Feng: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciated the support of Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434) and the Doctoral Science Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University (2016-B-13 and 2014-B-13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106420.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
